concepts.title: Vector Triple Product
concepts.conceptBody:
Vector Triple Product

If $\overrightarrow{\text{a}},\overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ are three vectors then $(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}})\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})$ are called vector triple products.

In general $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})\ne (\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}})\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$.

$\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ is perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})$ is perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$, therefore $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})$ is perpendicular to $\vec{a}$ and is parallel to $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$. Whereas $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is parallel to $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ and is perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$, thus $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})\ne (\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}})\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$.

For any three vectors $\vec{a},\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$

(i) $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})=(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{c}})\overrightarrow{\text{b}}-(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$

(ii) $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\cdot \vec{c}=(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{c})\vec{b}-(\vec{b}\cdot \vec{c})\vec{a}$

AID To Memory

$\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})=(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{c}})\overrightarrow{\text{b}}-(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ can also be wrotten as $\text{I}\times (\text{II}\times \text{III})=(\text{I}.\text{III})\text{II}-(\text{I}.\text{II})\text{III}$.

Note 1. The vector triple product $\vec{a}\times (\vec{b}\times \vec{c})$ is a linear combination of those two vectors which are within brackets.

Note 2. The vector $\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})$ is perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}$ and lies in the plane of $\overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$.

Note 3. The formula $\vec{a}\times (\vec{b}\times \vec{c})=(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{c})\vec{b}-(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b})\vec{c}$ is true only when the vector outside the bracket is on the left-most side. If it is not we first shift on left by using the properties of cross-product and then apply the same formula. For example,

$(\vec{b}\times \vec{c})\times \vec{a}=-(\vec{a}\times (\vec{b}\times \vec{c}))$

$=-\{(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{c})\vec{b}-(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b})\cdot \vec{c}\}=-(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{c})\vec{b}+(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b})\vec{c}$

concepts.title: Reciprocal of Vectors
concepts.conceptBody:
Reciprocal system of vectors

Let $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ be three non-coplanar vectors, and let

${{\vec{a}}^{\prime }}=\frac{\vec{b}\times \vec{c}}{[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]},{{\vec{b}}^{\prime }}=\frac{\vec{c}\times \vec{a}}{[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]},{{\vec{c}}^{\prime }}=\frac{\vec{a}\times \vec{b}}{[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]}$

$\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are said to form a reciprocal system of vectors for the vectors $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$.

Properties of Reciprocal System of Vectors

if $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ and $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ form a reciprocal system of vectors, then show that

(i) $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{a}=\vec{b}\cdot \vec{b}=\vec{c}\cdot \vec{c}=1$

(ii)$\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot {{\overrightarrow{\text{c}}}^{\prime }}=0;\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\cdot {{\overrightarrow{\text{c}}}^{\prime }}=\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{a}}=0;\overrightarrow{\text{c}}\cdot {{\overrightarrow{\text{a}}}^{\prime }}=\overrightarrow{\text{c}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}}=0$

(iii) $\left[ {{\overrightarrow{\text{a}}}^{\prime }}{{\overrightarrow{\text{b}}}^{\prime }}{{\overrightarrow{\text{c}}}^{\prime }} \right]=\frac{1}{[\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\overrightarrow{\text{c}}]}$

(iv) $\overrightarrow{\text{a}},\overrightarrow{\text{b}},\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ are non-coplanar iff so are ${{\overrightarrow{\text{a}}}^{\prime }},{{\overrightarrow{\text{b}}}^{\prime }},{{\overrightarrow{\text{c}}}^{\prime }}$.
